prompt: Regarding the attitude control problem for UAVs, most open-source flight controllers currently implement cascaded PID control algorithms. However, a single set of PID controller parameters typically performs well only under specific flight conditions. In practical applications, UAVs operate across diverse flight states. What methods can be employed to enhance the actual control performance of PID algorithms, and how should PID parameters be optimally selected?
article:
## 1) Why one fixed PID tune degrades across “diverse flight states” (what changes in the plant)

### 1.1 Fixed-wing: control effectiveness varies strongly with airspeed (dynamic pressure)
- Both **ArduPilot** and **PX4** explicitly document that **control-surface effectiveness increases/decreases with airspeed**, so a controller tuned at one speed will be too aggressive at high speed (oscillations) and too weak at low speed (poor tracking) unless gains are scaled/scheduled. ArduPilot: speed scaling is used “to prevent oscillations at higher airspeeds and preserve stabilization effectiveness at low airspeeds” [[57]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/airspeed-parameters-setup.html). PX4: without airspeed scaling, a cruise-tuned controller “will make the aircraft oscillate at higher airspeed” or track poorly at low airspeed [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams).  
- PX4 further states the **rate PI output should scale with IAS²** and the **rate feedforward should scale with TAS** [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams)—a concrete example that “one set of gains” is not physically consistent across speed.

### 1.2 Multirotors: thrust/moment authority is not constant (throttle, voltage, battery sag, payload)
- PX4 documents that the **PWM→thrust mapping depends highly on battery voltage** and recommends tuning around hover thrust; otherwise oscillations can appear near full throttle, motivating thrust-curve modeling/compensation [[117]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/config_mc/pid_tuning_guide_multicopter).  
- Many stacks therefore implement battery/thrust compensation to keep “effective plant gain” stable (e.g., PX4 battery scaling parameterization exists in the rate controller params [[6]](https://github.com/PX4/PX4-Autopilot/blob/master/src/modules/mc_rate_control/mc_rate_control_params.c), and hobby stacks use vbat sag compensation as a practical response-shaping tool [[38]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/guides/current/Freestyle-Tuning-Principles)).

### 1.3 Saturation, rate limits, and allocation change the closed-loop dynamics
- Cascaded attitude/rate control assumes the inner rate loop can realize commanded moments/rates. In reality, **actuator saturation** (motors at 0/100%, control surfaces at limits) and **allocation limits** change the system from linear to strongly nonlinear.
- PX4 explicitly highlights output limiting in allocation and the need to limit integral authority to prevent windup [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams). ArduPilot documents thrust-loss/yaw-imbalance as consequences of motors saturating such that the craft “can no longer achieve the requested roll, pitch, yaw and throttle output” [[48]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/thrust_loss_yaw_imbalance.html).  
- In saturation, a PID that “worked” in nominal conditions can overshoot, slow down, or even destabilize due to integrator windup and altered effective loop gain.

### 1.4 Noise, filtering, and delay vary by condition (and limit achievable D / bandwidth)
- Derivative action amplifies noise (PX4 explicitly warns high D makes motors twitchy/hot due to noise amplification [[117]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/config_mc/pid_tuning_guide_multicopter)).  
- Because vibration/noise spectrum changes with throttle, propwash, and airframe resonances, the amount of filtering required varies, and filtering introduces phase lag. Betaflight developers emphasize that **filter delay and motor time constants are often more important than ultra-high loop rates** for overall latency and performance [[123]](https://github.com/betaflight/betaflight/issues/7327).  
- Hence a “single tune” is rarely optimal across low-noise hover, high-throttle flight, propwash-heavy maneuvers, and payload configurations.

### 1.5 Disturbance environment changes (wind can be a huge fraction of airspeed)
- For small UAVs/MAVs, wind disturbances can be 20–60% of desired airspeed, directly limiting achievable tracking precision unless the control system is designed/tuned for it [[124]](http://www.et.byu.edu/~beard/papers/preprints/NelsonBarberMcLainBeard06.pdf). Gains that work in calm conditions may not reject wind well; gains that reject wind aggressively may excite noise/actuator limits in calm conditions.

---

## 2) What open-source flight controllers “actually implement” today (cascaded PID, plus practical add-ons)

Almost all mainstream open-source stacks implement:
- **Outer loop (attitude/angle)** → generates **body-rate setpoints**
- **Inner loop (rate)** → PID/PI(+FF) on gyro rates → torque/moment command → mixer/control allocation

### 2.1 PX4 (multicopter + fixed-wing)
- PX4 documents multicopter control as a **standard cascaded architecture** where the attitude controller (quaternion-based) produces a **rate command** and the rate controller is a **K-PID** producing torque commands; integrator authority is limited and outputs are limited in allocation [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams).
- Code-level reality: PX4 multicopter attitude module computes body-rate setpoints via `_attitude_control.update(...)` and uses per-axis proportional attitude gains (MC_ROLL_P, MC_PITCH_P, MC_YAW_P) [[2]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/d98/mc__att__control__main_8cpp_source.html).  
- PX4’s inner **RateControl** computes torque as **P(rate error) + I − D(d/dt of filtered rate) + FF(rate_sp)** [[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html), with:
  - **D-term low-pass filtering** (2-pole LPF vector) [[3]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d6/d3c/class_rate_control.html)[[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html)
  - **Saturation-aware anti-windup** using mixer saturation flags (clamping rate_error so I doesn’t integrate further into saturation) [[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html)
  - An additional **large-error I attenuation** to reduce flip “bounce-back” behavior [[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html)
- Fixed-wing: PX4 documents cascaded attitude→rate control and **airspeed scaling** (PI scaled with IAS², FF with TAS) to maintain consistent dynamics across speed [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams).

### 2.2 ArduPilot (Copter/Heli/Plane)
- ArduPilot Plane explicitly describes attitude stabilization as attitude error → demanded axial rate → **PID-FF rate loop**, with **FF providing most of the demanded rate** and PID correcting disturbances and errors [[9]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/new-roll-and-pitch-tuning.html).  
- ArduPilot’s generic PID library (**AC_PID**) includes many real-world “PID enhancements” directly in the primitive:
  - FF and **derivative feedforward (D_FF)**, target/error/derivative filters (FLTT/FLTE/FLTD), **slew limiting (SMAX)**, PD sum limits, and integrator management (IMAX clamp, reset_I, relax_integrator) [[12]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AC_PID/AC_PID.cpp).
- Multicopter attitude control exposes per-axis rate PID subgroups with FF, filter frequencies, slew limits, and notch filter indices [[13]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/raw/refs/heads/master/libraries/AC_AttitudeControl/AC_AttitudeControl_Multi.cpp).  
- ArduPilot also provides extensive integrator management options in practice (e.g., leaky integrator vs landing/takeoff reset strategies in heli) [[30]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/traditional-helicopter-tuning-other-topics.html) and warns explicitly about unsafe behavior if pitch integrator limits are too high (risk of stall) [[9]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/new-roll-and-pitch-tuning.html).

### 2.3 Betaflight / iNav (FPV-oriented multirotors; still “PID core,” heavy setpoint shaping)
- Betaflight evolved toward **explicit feedforward for stick responsiveness**, decoupling “responsiveness” from D damping. In BF 3.5: “Feed Forward now takes over the role of D setpoint weight” and is tuned per-axis [[20]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/3-5-tuning-notes).  
- Betaflight adds practical i-term controls:
  - iTerm Relax and windup controls to reduce bounce-back on low-authority builds by suppressing I accumulation during fast maneuvers or saturation-like conditions [[21]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/4-2-Tuning-Notes)[[37]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/guides/current/I-Term-Relax-Explained).
  - Anti-gravity (dynamic I boost) tied to throttle transients [[20]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/3-5-tuning-notes).
  - Many smoothing/averaging knobs for feedforward and filtering parameters in the PID profile [[22]](https://github.com/betaflight/betaflight/raw/refs/heads/master/src/main/flight/pid.c).
- iNav documents two controller variants:
  - Fixed-wing **PIFF** (no D) and multirotor **PIDCD** where “control derivative” is essentially a setpoint-derivative term (feedforward-like), plus Iterm Relax and explicit anti-windup/back-calculation components in the I update [[15]](https://github.com/iNavFlight/inav/blob/master/docs/INAV%20PID%20Controller.md)[[16]](https://github.com/iNavFlight/inav/raw/refs/heads/master/docs/INAV%20PID%20Controller.md).

### 2.4 Paparazzi
- Paparazzi documents that it uses PID across many loops but often doesn’t use full I/D everywhere due to tuning/necessity [[24]](https://wiki.paparazziuav.org/wiki/Theory_of_Operation), and its fixed-wing stabilization code includes throttle-dependent gain scheduling in roll rate loop [[25]](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/paparazzi/paparazzi/master/sw/airborne/firmwares/fixedwing/stabilization/stabilization_attitude.c).  
- Paparazzi rotorcraft controllers include options beyond PID (e.g., INDI is explicitly available as a stabilization implementation) [[26]](https://wiki.paparazziuav.org/wiki/Subsystem/stabilization).

**Takeaway:** modern “PID” flight stacks are rarely plain PID; they are PID plus *feedforward, saturation logic, integrator rules, filtering, and setpoint shaping*.

---

## 3) Methods to improve real-world control performance while “staying PID”

Below are the highest-impact methods used in practice (many already present in PX4/ArduPilot/BF, but often underused or mis-tuned).

### 3.1 Make the inner loop trackable: feedforward + decouple responsiveness from damping
**Goal:** reduce reliance on feedback error; maintain crisp response without pushing P/D into noise/oscillation.

- **Rate feedforward** (commanded-rate to torque/moment) improves tracking and reduces phase-lag dependence. PX4 implements torque += FF * rate_sp [[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html) and exposes MC_*RATE_FF parameters [[6]](https://github.com/PX4/PX4-Autopilot/blob/master/src/modules/mc_rate_control/mc_rate_control_params.c). ArduPilot Plane emphasizes FF can supply most demanded rate [[9]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/new-roll-and-pitch-tuning.html).  
- **Derivative feedforward / setpoint-derivative terms** (where supported) improve fast setpoint changes without destabilizing D-on-measurement. ArduPilot AC_PID exposes D_FF [[12]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AC_PID/AC_PID.cpp) and the rate PID groups expose RAT_*_D_FF [[13]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/raw/refs/heads/master/libraries/AC_AttitudeControl/AC_AttitudeControl_Multi.cpp).
- Betaflight’s explicit feedforward is precisely this “responsiveness” channel, leaving D mainly for damping/noise control [[20]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/3-5-tuning-notes).

**Practical tuning implication:**  
1) Set FF to get most of the commanded maneuver “open-loop,”  
2) Use P to correct short-term mismatch,  
3) Use I to remove steady biases,  
4) Use D only as needed for damping (limited by noise and delay).

### 3.2 Anti-windup that matches UAV reality (allocation, saturation, and mode transitions)
Integral action is essential for bias rejection (CG offsets, thrust asymmetry, wind), but it must be *disciplined*.

Common, effective patterns:
- **Conditional integration / clamping with saturation flags:** PX4 clamps the sign of rate_error integrated when mixer indicates positive/negative saturation [[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html). This is extremely relevant to multirotors.  
- **Integrator limits (IMAX) and axis-specific rules:** widely exposed in ArduPilot and used as safety constraints [[9]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/new-roll-and-pitch-tuning.html)[[12]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AC_PID/AC_PID.cpp)[[13]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/raw/refs/heads/master/libraries/AC_AttitudeControl/AC_AttitudeControl_Multi.cpp).  
- **Integrator leak / relaxation:** ArduPilot provides relax_integrator() to smoothly reduce I under dynamic conditions or at transitions [[12]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AC_PID/AC_PID.cpp), and documents “leaky integrator” vs “landing/takeoff detection reset” strategies in heli [[30]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/traditional-helicopter-tuning-other-topics.html).  
- **Large-error I suppression for aerobatics/step inputs:** PX4 reduces effective I update at very large rate errors to mitigate flip bounce-back [[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html); Betaflight’s iTerm Relax similarly suppresses I during fast maneuvers [[21]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/4-2-Tuning-Notes)[[37]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/guides/current/I-Term-Relax-Explained).

**Practical tuning implication:** choose I based on disturbance rejection and steady offsets, then ensure anti-windup rules prevent I from fighting actuator limits during aggressive maneuvers and low-authority states.

### 3.3 Setpoint conditioning (input shaping) and rate/accel limiting
A large fraction of “PID problems” are actually *setpoint problems*: commanding discontinuous steps that excite structural modes and saturate actuators.

- ArduPilot explicitly separates “input shaping” (e.g., ATC_INPUT_TC and acceleration limits) from raw PID gains so you can keep a strong tune but a docile feel [[11]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/input-shaping.html)[[14]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/raw/refs/heads/master/libraries/AC_AttitudeControl/AC_AttitudeControl.cpp).  
- PX4 and ArduPilot both impose rate/accel limits in various places (e.g., Paparazzi includes slew limiting in course/roll loops [[25]](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/paparazzi/paparazzi/master/sw/airborne/firmwares/fixedwing/stabilization/stabilization_attitude.c); ArduPilot AC_PID has SMAX slew limiting [[12]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AC_PID/AC_PID.cpp)).  
- Betaflight uses extensive RC smoothing and feedforward transition shaping [[20]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/3-5-tuning-notes)[[22]](https://github.com/betaflight/betaflight/raw/refs/heads/master/src/main/flight/pid.c).

**Practical tuning implication:** if your aircraft must handle both gentle filming and aggressive maneuvers, you often want *two regimes of setpoint shaping* more than two sets of PID gains.

### 3.4 Filtering and notch strategies to expand the stable gain region (less lag for same noise)
Because D and high P are limited by noise and delay, improved filtering is often the easiest way to “raise performance.”

- PX4 describes an IMU pipeline with notch + LPF, and derivative filtering via IMU_DGYRO_CUTOFF [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams); the RateControl D path uses filtered rate differentiation [[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html).  
- ArduPilot documents dynamic harmonic notch filtering linked to motor rotational frequencies and even in-flight FFT-based methods; the key motivation is to allow higher gyro lowpass (less phase lag) while removing motor peaks [[40]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-imu-notch-filtering.html). It warns about CPU cost if you enable too many notches/harmonics [[40]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-imu-notch-filtering.html).  
- Betaflight emphasizes the delay tradeoff: filters reduce noise but “all filters induce some delay,” and modern approaches include dynamic lowpass and RPM-based filtering to remove motor noise with less lag [[39]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/4-0-Tuning-Notes).

**Practical tuning implication:** treat filters as part of the controller design. “Optimal PID” without filter design is usually impossible on real frames.

### 3.5 Actuator linearization and thrust modeling (reduce plant nonlinearity so PID sees something consistent)
- ArduPilot provides thrust curve linearization via MOT_THST_EXPO and MOT_SPIN_MAX, warning that ESCs with built-in linearization may require lower expo [[42]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/motor-thrust-scaling.html).  
- PX4 discusses thrust modeling via a factor in thrust = (1−factor)*PWM + factor*PWM² (THR_MDL_FAC) in practice contexts [[44]](https://discuss.px4.io/t/thrust-curve-compensation-through-thr-mdl-fac/13207) and recommends thrust curve parameter adjustments to handle high-thrust oscillations [[117]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/config_mc/pid_tuning_guide_multicopter).  
- These methods reduce throttle-dependent loop gain variation, improving stability across throttle.

### 3.6 Better limit handling and axis prioritization (control allocation aware tuning)
Because multirotors can’t always satisfy roll/pitch/yaw/thrust simultaneously, the “effective plant” depends on prioritization logic.
- PX4’s control allocation is a distinct module translating torque/thrust into actuator commands for a given geometry [[45]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/concept/control_allocation), and PX4’s mixer contains explicit desaturation logic [[46]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d5/dd1/class_multirotor_mixer.html).  
- Monitoring and using saturation information (as PX4 does for anti-windup [[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html)) is key to avoiding integrator-driven oscillations and bounce-back.

---

## 4) Gain scheduling (multi-regime PID) to cover diverse flight states

“Enhancing PID performance” across the envelope usually means **gain scheduling + envelope-aware filters/FF**, not a single static PID.

### 4.1 What to schedule on (common, high-value scheduling variables)
- **Fixed-wing:** airspeed / dynamic pressure is the canonical scheduling variable. ArduPilot’s speed scaling uses AIRSPEED_MIN/MAX and SCALING_SPEED and warns that changing SCALING_SPEED after tuning corrupts the tune [[57]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/airspeed-parameters-setup.html). PX4 specifies PI scaled with IAS² and FF scaled with TAS [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams).  
- **Multirotor:** common scheduling signals include:
  - **Throttle / motor RPM proxy** (because vibration and authority scale with RPM/throttle; Betaflight’s dynamic filtering and TPA-like concepts embody this [[39]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/4-0-Tuning-Notes)[[127]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/guides/current/PID-Tuning-Guide)).
  - **Battery voltage / sag estimate** (PX4 battery scaling exists [[6]](https://github.com/PX4/PX4-Autopilot/blob/master/src/modules/mc_rate_control/mc_rate_control_params.c); BF vbat compensation exists conceptually [[38]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/guides/current/Freestyle-Tuning-Principles)).  
  - **Mass/inertia estimate / payload state** (harder to measure directly, but practically many teams use “payload mode” gain banks or schedule on takeoff weight estimates).
  - **Tilt angle / aerodynamic regime** (for aggressive forward flight, drag and coupling increase; this is often handled by higher-level logic plus setpoint shaping).

### 4.2 How to build schedules (from pragmatic to formal)
1) **Physics-based scaling laws where available**  
   - Fixed-wing: scale by dynamic pressure as PX4 prescribes (IAS² for PI, TAS for FF) [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams).  
2) **Lookup tables (gain maps) + smooth interpolation**  
   - Common implementation: 1D schedule (airspeed or throttle), possibly 2D (airspeed × flap setting; throttle × RPM filter state).  
   - Critical detail: ensure **continuous interpolation** and **bounded rate of change** of gains to avoid exciting the loop with “gain steps.” LPV literature warns ad-hoc interpolation can destabilize controllers [[59]](https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/20030002264/downloads/20030002264.pdf)[[58]](https://ntrs.nasa.gov/citations/20030002264).  
3) **LPV / multi-model design viewpoint (when you need guarantees)**  
   - LPV controller interpolation methods explicitly aim for continuous gains and global stability/performance certificates across parameter space via Lyapunov constructions [[58]](https://ntrs.nasa.gov/citations/20030002264)[[59]](https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/20030002264/downloads/20030002264.pdf). This is usually overkill for hobby UAVs, but relevant for professional envelope expansion.

### 4.3 Validate schedules (don’t just “make tables”)
Use classical robustness checks at several operating points and in transitions:
- NASA robustness guidance gives typical flight-control targets like ±6 dB gain margin and ±45° phase margin [[82]](https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/20240014107/downloads/2025_BodeIntegralv4.pdf).  
- Aerospace standards/guidelines also emphasize analyzing flexible modes, delays, and uncertainties as part of margin verification [[132]](https://standards.nasa.gov/sites/default/files/standards/GSFC/H/0/GSFC-STD-1000RevH_Approved.pdf).  
- In practice: evaluate margins and time responses at min/nominal/max airspeed, payload states, battery states, and then test transition cases where scheduling variables change quickly.

---

## 5) Adaptive and auto-tuning methods (online help) + safety tradeoffs

### 5.1 What is used in open-source stacks today
- **ArduPilot AutoTune (Copter):** attempts to tune Stabilize P, Rate P & D, and max rotational accelerations for “highest response without significant overshoot,” but explicitly warns it can produce “unflyable” gains and a crash; recommends good notch filtering and pre-tuning to reduce risk [[76]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/autotune.html).  
- **ArduPilot AutoTune (Plane):** learns tuning from sharp attitude changes and has aggressiveness levels; includes guidance to mitigate oscillations by increasing loop rate and reducing filtering delay, with warnings about noise and the need for notch filtering [[61]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/automatic-tuning-with-autotune.html).  
- **PX4 Autotune via QGroundControl:** includes a pre-test criterion (stabilize within 2 oscillations up to ~20°) before enabling autotune [[77]](https://docs.qgroundcontrol.com/Stable_V4.3/en/qgc-user-guide/setup_view/tuning_px4.html). PX4 fixed-wing applies gains in flight and performs a test with automatic reversion on problems, while multicopters apply after landing/disarming [[77]](https://docs.qgroundcontrol.com/Stable_V4.3/en/qgc-user-guide/setup_view/tuning_px4.html).

**Key point:** autopilot autotuners are valuable but are *not* magic; they require adequate mechanical condition, noise control, and safe fallback.

### 5.2 System identification-driven retuning (more systematic, but more engineering)
- **ArduPilot System Identification Mode:** injects chirp signals at defined points in attitude/position loops, logging data for frequency-domain characterization, model generation (digital twins), and evaluation of tune quality [[62]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-systemid-mode.html)[[136]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-systemid-mode-operation.html).  
- This enables: identify models at multiple flight states → design gains (loop shaping / optimization) → validate → schedule.

### 5.3 Optimization-based tuning (PSO/CMA-ES/BO) as offline/online tools
- PSO is widely used in research to tune PID gains by minimizing trajectory errors (SSE-style metrics), showing improvements vs baseline manual parameters in simulation studies [[68]](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10615089)[[69]](https://scholarworks.utrgv.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi)[[70]](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-4591/90/1/2).  
- A 2025 preprint compares gradient-free metaheuristics, Bayesian optimization, and RL for PID tuning; it reports metaheuristics improve consistently, BO is sample-efficient but overhead-heavy, and RL did not beat baseline in their setup [[73]](https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.17423).  
- These methods become practical when paired with strong safety constraints, simulation-to-real workflows, and robust validation.

---

## 6) Robust “augmentations” that keep PID (or a baseline loop) but improve envelope robustness

If you want to keep cascaded PID as the core structure, augmentations can reduce sensitivity to disturbances/model changes without turning the whole system into MPC/backstepping.

### 6.1 Disturbance observers (DOB) layered on PID
- A quadrotor DOBC study explicitly describes **disturbance observer based control designed to compensate a nominal conventional PID**, “patching on the existing controller,” and validates with simulation and flight experiments on a DJI MATRICE 100 with wind/aero disturbance data [[88]](https://www.imavs.org/papers/2018/46.pdf).  
**Mechanism:** estimate lumped torque disturbance → add compensating torque feedforward; keep PID for nominal response.

### 6.2 ADRC / ESO-style disturbance estimation
- An ISA Transactions practice article surveys observer-based disturbance rejection (ESO/NDO/UIO/UDE etc.) and proposes an adaptive composite disturbance rejection control for quadrotor attitude under wind/payload/prop faults with experiments and large reported improvements [[94]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0019057822000180).  
**Tradeoff:** stronger disturbance rejection, but ESO bandwidth/tuning becomes a key knob; they even propose online tuning via ILC [[94]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0019057822000180).

### 6.3 L1 adaptive augmentation (“wrap around” a stable baseline)
- An MIT AIAA paper emphasizes L1 can be wrapped around an already-stable closed-loop system using an input-output model, separating performance and robustness via a low-pass filter, validated in flight tests on an indoor quadrotor testbed [[106]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/57900/How_L1Adaptive.pdf).  
- A quadrotor L1 augmentation preprint reports substantially smaller tracking errors with experiments and provides an open-source code link [[107]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2109.06998).  
**Tradeoff:** more design complexity (choose filter bandwidth and adaptation rate), but can handle wider uncertainty without hand-building gain schedules.

### 6.4 INDI (often a replacement for inner loops, but relevant as an alternative)
- INDI is often not “PID core,” but it’s relevant because it addresses the same envelope problem: robustness to model mismatch/disturbances. A CJA paper reports NDI outer loop + INDI inner angular-acceleration loop with simulations and flight tests, compared with PID [[100]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1000936121001096).  
- Practical guidance emphasizes sensitivity to delays and the need for careful filtering/synchronization [[101]](https://repository.tudelft.nl/file/File_72437d3f-ba51-4718-be28-a368de7d02f3).  
**Tradeoff:** potentially excellent robustness, but more stringent requirements on timing, acceleration estimation, and actuator modeling.

---

## 7) How to select PID parameters “optimally” across diverse flight states (a best-practice workflow)

### Step A — Define what “optimal” means (multi-objective, with constraints)
A defensible “optimal PID” for UAV attitude control is never a single metric; it’s a constrained multi-objective design:

**Core objectives**
- Attitude/rate tracking bandwidth and rise time
- Overshoot / oscillation limits (e.g., “settle within N oscillations,” similar to PX4’s pre-test logic [[77]](https://docs.qgroundcontrol.com/Stable_V4.3/en/qgc-user-guide/setup_view/tuning_px4.html))
- Disturbance rejection (wind, CG offsets, propwash)

**Robustness constraints**
- Gain/phase margins (NASA notes typical ±6 dB and ±45° as common flight-control robustness requirements) [[82]](https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/20240014107/downloads/2025_BodeIntegralv4.pdf)
- Include delays and flexible modes (aerospace standards emphasize analyzing sample/data delay and flexible mode effects with uncertainties) [[132]](https://standards.nasa.gov/sites/default/files/standards/GSFC/H/0/GSFC-STD-1000RevH_Approved.pdf)
- Sensitivity peak constraints (control literature frames robustness constraints via sensitivity peaks Ms/Mt) [[130]](https://skoge.folk.ntnu.no/publications/thesis/2018_grimholt/phd-thesis-grimholt-2018.pdf)

**Implementation constraints**
- Actuator saturation frequency and authority (avoid sustained saturation; watch thrust-loss/yaw-imbalance risk) [[48]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/thrust_loss_yaw_imbalance.html)
- Noise amplification and motor heating limits (D-term and high bandwidth limited by noise; PX4 explicitly notes D amplifies noise and can heat motors) [[117]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/config_mc/pid_tuning_guide_multicopter)
- CPU budget for filters/notches (ArduPilot warns excessive harmonic notches can overload CPU) [[40]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-imu-notch-filtering.html)

### Step B — Identify dynamics (at multiple operating points) rather than tuning blind
Pick the least risky method your tooling supports:

1) **Chirp / frequency sweep identification (preferred when available)**
- Use ArduPilot SYSID chirps at defined injection points to produce frequency-domain data and digital twins [[62]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-systemid-mode.html)[[136]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-systemid-mode-operation.html).

2) **PRBS and subspace/black-box identification (research-grade but practical)**
- Example methodology: design PRBS to cover expected attitude bandwidth; handle delays explicitly; validate on separate data segments [[138]](https://dspace-erf.nlr.nl/server/api/core/bitstreams/3a7f86f5-66a8-4a71-8a2c-997a1b8eb7ba/content).

3) **Relay feedback experiments (classic autotuning identification)**
- Relay autotuning literature explains relay methods reduce risk of driving the loop unstable compared to Ziegler–Nichols cycling, while identifying useful frequency points [[135]](https://warwick.ac.uk/fac/cross_fac/iatl/research/reinvention/archive/volume5issue2/hornsey/).

### Step C — Design/tune in the frequency domain (loop shaping) or constrained optimization
Two complementary approaches:

**(1) Loop shaping / margin-based design**
- Use Bode/Nichols to target crossover and phase margin; ensure filtering delay is compatible with desired bandwidth (Betaflight notes filter delay can dominate) [[123]](https://github.com/betaflight/betaflight/issues/7327).  
- For fixed-wing, incorporate airspeed scaling laws explicitly (PX4 IAS² / TAS scaling) [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams) and ArduPilot SCALING_SPEED discipline [[57]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/airspeed-parameters-setup.html).

**(2) Constrained optimization on a simulation+identified model**
- Use cost functions like integrated error for disturbances and tracking, constrained by robustness metrics (e.g., sensitivity peaks Ms/Mt as in optimal PID research) [[130]](https://skoge.folk.ntnu.no/publications/thesis/2018_grimholt/phd-thesis-grimholt-2018.pdf).
- For black-box search (PSO/CMA-ES/BO), enforce hard constraints: margins, saturation limits, maximum control effort, noise amplification. PSO-based PID tuning for quadrotor has shown improvements in simulation metrics [[68]](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10615089)[[69]](https://scholarworks.utrgv.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi)[[70]](https://www.mdpi.com/2673-4591/90/1/2), but must be constrained for real-world safety.

### Step D — Add scheduling (if one controller cannot satisfy all regimes)
- Fixed-wing: schedule on airspeed/dynamic pressure; follow established scaling (PX4) [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams) or ArduPilot’s speed scaling parameters (AIRSPEED_MIN/MAX, SCALING_SPEED) and tune at the center speed [[57]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/airspeed-parameters-setup.html).  
- Multirotor: schedule on throttle/RPM proxy, battery voltage, payload mode; combine with setpoint shaping so “feel” remains consistent across modes.

### Step E — Validate systematically (simulation → Monte Carlo → HIL → flight)
- Monte Carlo and HIL are standard in aerospace GN&C verification workflows (NASA labs routinely run thousands of Monte Carlo simulations and HIL) [[133]](https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2015/07/2019_07_ea_gnc.pdf).  
- NASA safety guidance emphasizes sensitivity studies, stressing cases, and not relying solely on flight data to “validate margins” because excitation may be insufficient [[131]](https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/20220009857/downloads/Ascent%20Stability.pdf).  
- PX4/ArduPilot autotune procedures embed practical safety validation: readiness tests, abort logic, and in some cases automatic reversion [[77]](https://docs.qgroundcontrol.com/Stable_V4.3/en/qgc-user-guide/setup_view/tuning_px4.html)[[76]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/autotune.html).

---

## 8) Concrete, actionable recommendations (what to do in practice)

### 8.1 If you are staying with cascaded PID (typical PX4/ArduPilot/BF approach)
1) **Start by fixing noise and delays** (mounting, balancing, notch strategy) because it expands the feasible PID region [[40]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-imu-notch-filtering.html)[[39]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/4-0-Tuning-Notes).  
2) **Tune the rate loop first** (PX4 explicitly orders tuning this way [[78]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/config_mc/pid_tuning_guide_multicopter_basic)); add **rate feedforward** (PX4/ArduPilot/BF) before pushing P/D too high [[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html)[[9]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/new-roll-and-pitch-tuning.html)[[20]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/3-5-tuning-notes).  
3) Implement/enable **saturation-aware anti-windup** (or ensure it’s correctly configured); use integrator limits and conditional integration; reset/relax I on mode transitions [[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html)[[12]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AC_PID/AC_PID.cpp)[[30]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/traditional-helicopter-tuning-other-topics.html).  
4) Apply **actuator/thrust linearization** and battery compensation so the same gains behave similarly across voltage/throttle [[42]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/motor-thrust-scaling.html)[[117]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/config_mc/pid_tuning_guide_multicopter).  
5) Add **gain scheduling** where physics demands it:
   - Fixed-wing: airspeed scaling is non-negotiable for wide envelopes [[57]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/airspeed-parameters-setup.html)[[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams).
   - Multirotor: throttle/RPM-dependent damping (TPA/dynamic damping) and possibly voltage scheduling are often required [[127]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/guides/current/PID-Tuning-Guide)[[38]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/guides/current/Freestyle-Tuning-Principles).  
6) Use **setpoint shaping** (input time constants, accel limits, feedforward transition shaping) to get “dual personality” behavior without destabilizing the core tune [[11]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/input-shaping.html)[[20]](https://betaflight.com/docs/wiki/tuning/3-5-tuning-notes)[[14]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/raw/refs/heads/master/libraries/AC_AttitudeControl/AC_AttitudeControl.cpp).

### 8.2 If your envelope is truly wide (payload swaps, strong winds, VTOL transitions)
- Prefer **scheduled gains + identification-based validation**, or consider **robust augmentation** (DOB/ESO/L1) layered on your tuned PID baseline:
  - DOB-on-PID has direct flight validation evidence on a quadrotor [[88]](https://www.imavs.org/papers/2018/46.pdf).
  - L1 augmentation has flight-test evidence and an explicit “wrap-around stable loop” design concept [[106]](https://dspace.mit.edu/bitstream/handle/1721.1/57900/How_L1Adaptive.pdf)[[107]](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2109.06998).
  - ADRC/ESO approaches show strong disturbance rejection but require careful observer bandwidth tuning [[94]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0019057822000180).

---

## Bottom line
To enhance real control performance of PID attitude controllers across diverse flight states, you generally do **not** replace PID; you (1) **improve the PID “ecosystem”** (feedforward, anti-windup, filtering, actuator linearization, setpoint shaping, allocation-aware limits) and (2) **move from one fixed tune to multi-regime tuning** via **gain scheduling and/or adaptive augmentation**, grounded in **system identification** and validated with **robustness margins, uncertainty testing, and staged flight-test protocols** [[28]](https://docs.px4.io/main/en/flight_stack/controller_diagrams)[[4]](https://px4.github.io/Firmware-Doxygen/d4/dee/_rate_control_8cpp_source.html)[[12]](https://github.com/ArduPilot/ardupilot/blob/master/libraries/AC_PID/AC_PID.cpp)[[40]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-imu-notch-filtering.html)[[57]](https://ardupilot.org/plane/docs/airspeed-parameters-setup.html)[[76]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/autotune.html)[[77]](https://docs.qgroundcontrol.com/Stable_V4.3/en/qgc-user-guide/setup_view/tuning_px4.html)[[62]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-systemid-mode.html)[[136]](https://ardupilot.org/copter/docs/common-systemid-mode-operation.html)[[82]](https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/20240014107/downloads/2025_BodeIntegralv4.pdf)[[133]](https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2015/07/2019_07_ea_gnc.pdf)[[131]](https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/20220009857/downloads/Ascent%20Stability.pdf).
